name: Spritely Goblins — Object-Capability Library for Scheme
summary: Spritely Institute's ocap library for Racket and Guile Scheme. Implements CapTP and 'time-traveling' transactional ocap actors with quasi-functional actor turns enabling local rollback. Netlayer abstraction over Tor / I2P / libp2p; Hyptis / Brassica demonstrate composing ocap with CRDTs. Spritely Institute is a funded non-profit; Goblins is in beta as of 2026-04.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
**Spritely Goblins** is the Spritely Institute's object-capability library for Racket and Guile Scheme — a direct port of E's vat / eventual-send / CapTP model into the Scheme ecosystem. It is the most actively developed modern descendant of E and the reference implementation for the OCapN cross-language network protocol.

Goblins is implemented as a **library**, not a separate language: a Racket or Guile program loads `spritely/goblins`, instantiates a vat, and is now writing ocap-discipline code on a standard Scheme runtime. This is the contrast with Monte and Newspeak (standalone runtimes) — Goblins gets ocap discipline at the cost of Scheme conventions for everything else, but inherits Scheme's mature tooling and ecosystem.

sections.title: Transactional actor turns and 'time travel'
sections.content:
Goblins's distinctive technical contribution is **quasi-functional actor turns** that enable local rollback — informally, "time travel." Each actor turn is treated transactionally: state changes are deferred until the turn commits, and a turn that fails (raises, hits a timeout, gets explicitly aborted) leaves no observable trace.

This matters for ocap distributed systems for two reasons:

- **Composability of failure-handling**: an actor can speculatively run a sub-computation, observe whether it succeeds, and either commit or roll back without leaking partial state.
- **Auditable replay**: the deterministic-by-default turn log can be replayed for debugging or reproducibility, supporting the kind of `dotnet-edit-and-continue`-style live debugging that is otherwise hard to bolt onto an actor system.

Goblins is also the canonical implementation venue for **OCapN** (the cross-implementation ocap-networking standard being co-developed with Agoric); the netlayer abstraction supports running Goblins peers over **Tor**, **I2P**, **libp2p**, or direct TCP — selecting transport per deployment without the application code knowing.

sections.title: Ecosystem — Hyptis, Brassica, and current status
sections.content:
Status (as of 2026-04): **Spritely Institute** is a funded non-profit; **Goblins is in beta** with regular releases and a growing demo suite. The institute also funds related ocap work:

- **Hyptis** — demonstrates composing ocap with CRDTs (conflict-free replicated data types) for collaborative editing under capability discipline. CRDT merge becomes a per-capability operation, so parties without authority cannot affect the converged state.
- **Brassica** — a Goblins-on-Wasm port; the experiment of "can ocap actors run inside a WASI Component?" The motivation is composing the language-level ocap discipline (Goblins) with the binary-level ocap discipline (WASI Component Model) so the two work together rather than competing.
- **Goblins JS** — a JavaScript port (in progress) that interoperates with `capn-web` and the broader Hardened JS ecosystem.

Goblins is currently the most useful entry point for working ocap programmers: it is implemented in a mainstream language family (Scheme), it has tutorials and active community support, and it interoperates with Cap'n Proto / CapTP / OCapN for cross-system work.
